pb1_s1_pt.text: Excuse me, where is the X-ray room?

pb1_s1_p0.text: The X-ray room is on the second floor.

pb1_s1_p3.text: I'm not sure — let me find someone to help.

pb1_s2_p1.text: The doctor is with another patient right now.

pb1_s2_p2.text: We're running a little behind schedule today.

pb1_s2_p3.text: Please have a seat — we'll call your name.

pb1_s3_p0.text: Excuse me, can I help you?

pb1_s3_p3.text: Please come with me — I'll help you.

pb1_s3_p4.text: Let me get a nurse for you right away.

pb1_s4_p0.text: Let me find the pharmacist for you.

pb1_s4_p1.text: I'd suggest asking the doctor directly.

pb1_s4_p3.text: Your appointment is on Monday — let me confirm.

pb1_q0.text: Patient: "Where is the elevator?" — Best response:

pb1_q1.text: "We're running a little behind schedule today" means:

pb1_q2.text: You see a patient in the hallway, looking worried. What should you say first?

pb1_q3.text: Patient asks about their appointment but you're not managing their case. What do you say?

pb1_q4.text: Which phrase do you use when you physically walk WITH the patient to show them the way?

pb2_s1_p1.text: Gastritis means inflammation of the stomach.

pb2_s1_p2.text: You may have heard of hepatitis — that's inflammation of the liver.

pb2_s1_p3.text: "-itis" often signals inflammation in many common terms — context confirms it.

pb2_s2_p0.text: The root "cardio-" or "-cardia" refers to the heart.

pb2_s2_p1.text: Tachycardia means a fast heart rate — "tachy-" means fast.

pb2_s2_p2.text: Bradycardia is the opposite — a slow heart rate.

pb2_s2_p3.text: Cardiac arrest means the heart has stopped completely.

pb2_s3_pt.text: "Appendectomy" — can you tell what will happen just from the name?

pb2_s3_p1.text: An appendectomy is the removal of the appendix.

pb2_s3_p2.text: A cholecystectomy removes the gallbladder — "chole" = bile, "cyst" = sac.

pb2_s3_p3.text: The root gives a strong first guess about what is being removed; context confirms it.

pb2_s4_pt.text: "Referred to a gastroenterologist" — who exactly is that?

pb2_s4_p0.text: "-ology" means the study of — and "-ologist" is the specialist.

pb2_s4_p1.text: A gastroenterologist specializes in the digestive system.

pb2_s4_p3.text: The root gives a strong first guess about which organ that specialist treats; context confirms it.

pb2_q0.text: What does the suffix "-itis" mean in medical English?

pb2_q2.text: A patient is scheduled for a "nephrectomy." Which organ is involved?

pb2_q3.text: Which of these words means "inflammation of the liver"?

pb2_q4.text: A referral says "see a pulmonologist." This doctor specializes in:

pb3_s1_p0.text: Hello, I'm Dr. Minh. I'll be taking care of you today.

pb3_s1_p1.text: Good morning — my name is Dr. Lan from the internal medicine department.

pb3_s1_p2.text: Hi, I'm Dr. Nam, the resident on duty this morning. Nice to meet you.

pb3_s1_p3.text: Please don't worry — I'm Dr. Pham, and I'm here to help.

pb3_s2_pt.text: Well... I've been having some problems. I'm not really sure where to start.

pb3_s2_p1.text: How can I help you today?

pb3_s2_p2.text: Take your time — what's been going on?

pb3_s2_p3.text: What seems to be bothering you?

pb3_s3_pt.text: The pain started about three days ago. It sort of comes and goes... it's hard to describe, really.

pb3_s3_p0.text: Can you tell me more about that?

pb3_s3_p1.text: I see — and where exactly is the pain?

pb3_s3_p3.text: When you say 'comes and goes' — how often does that happen?

pb3_s4_pt.text: So... what are you going to do now? Is everything okay?

pb3_s4_p0.text: I'm going to examine you now. Is that okay?

pb3_s4_p1.text: First, I'll ask a few more questions, then do a physical exam.

pb3_s4_p2.text: Let's take it one step at a time — I'll check a few things first.

pb3_s4_p3.text: After the exam, we'll talk about what I found and the next steps.

pb3_q0.text: Which phrase correctly introduces yourself to a patient for the first time?

pb3_q1.text: Why is 'What brings you in today?' better than 'Do you have chest pain?'

pb3_q2.text: A patient stops talking mid-sentence, looking uncertain. Which response is best?

pb3_q3.text: After taking a history, the patient asks: 'What happens now?' Which reply is most appropriate?

pb3_q4.text: What is the clinical term for the main reason a patient visits a doctor today?

pb4_s1_p1.text: That's within the normal range — nothing to worry about.

pb4_s1_p2.text: The top number is your systolic pressure, and the bottom is diastolic.

pb4_s1_p3.text: Your BP is a little high — I'll check it again in a few minutes.

pb4_s2_pt.text: Can you give me a quick update on the vitals?

pb4_s2_p0.text: BP is 130 over 85, HR 88 beats per minute.

pb4_s2_p1.text: Respiratory rate is 18 breaths per minute, temperature 37.0 degrees Celsius.

pb4_s2_p2.text: SpO2 is 97 percent on room air.

pb4_s2_p3.text: All vitals are stable — no acute concerns.

pb4_s3_pt.text: Are those numbers okay? What do they mean?

pb4_s3_p0.text: Your oxygen saturation is 98 percent on room air — that's very good.

pb4_s3_p1.text: Your temperature is 37.0 degrees Celsius — perfectly normal.

pb4_s3_p2.text: SpO2 above 95 percent is what we want to see — yours is fine.

pb4_s3_p3.text: The temperature is slightly elevated at 38.1 — I'll keep monitoring.

pb4_s4_pt.text: That beeping machine — is something wrong with my heart?

pb4_s4_p0.text: Your heart rate is a little fast at 108 — that can happen when you're anxious or in pain.

pb4_s4_p1.text: The normal range is 60 to 100 beats per minute — yours is slightly above that.

pb4_s4_p2.text: I'll monitor it closely — please try to relax and take slow, deep breaths.

pb4_s4_p3.text: I'm going to let the doctor know about this reading.

pb4_q0.text: How do you correctly read '132/84 mmHg' aloud in clinical English?

pb4_q1.text: SpO2 is 93% on room air. Which response is most appropriate?

pb4_q2.text: A nurse says: 'RR is 22.' What does this mean?

pb4_q3.text: Which sentence uses the most appropriate register when explaining a normal temperature to a patient?

pb4_q4.text: A patient asks why you keep checking their blood pressure. What is the best explanation?

1a-01_s1_p1.text: Every word in this family is about the heart.

1a-01_s1_p2.text: Find the root first, then decode the rest.

1a-01_s2_p2.text: The suffix tells you what is happening to that body part.

1a-01_s3_p0.text: Use the combining vowel when the suffix starts with a consonant.

1a-01_s3_p1.text: Drop it when the suffix starts with a vowel.

1a-01_s3_p2.text: cardi + o + logy, but cardi + itis.

1a-01_s4_p0.text: Read the suffix first, then the root.

1a-01_s4_p1.text: Hepatitis means inflammation of the liver.

1a-01_s4_p2.text: A neurologist is a doctor who studies the nervous system.

1a-01_q0.text: Which three pieces build most medical words?

1a-01_q1.text: In 'hepatology', what does the root 'hepat' mean?

1a-02_s1_pt.text: "My throat has been burning for two days, doctor."

1a-02_s1_p1.text: Gastritis is inflammation of the stomach.

1a-02_s1_p2.text: Inflamed tissue is usually red, hot, swollen and painful.

1a-02_s2_p1.text: It is usually not an inflammation.

1a-02_s2_p2.text: Osteoporosis is a condition of porous, fragile bone.

1a-02_s3_pt.text: "The blood test shows you have anemia — that's why you feel tired."

1a-02_s3_p1.text: Anemia means the blood lacks healthy red cells.

1a-02_s3_p2.text: Notice the prefix: an- means without.

1a-02_s4_pt.text: "I have this aching pain in my muscles all over, doctor."

1a-02_s4_p2.text: Hepatomegaly means the liver is enlarged.

1a-02_q2.text: A tired patient's test shows 'anemia'. The suffix tells you the problem is in…

1a-02_q4.text: You palpate an enlarged spleen (splen = spleen). The term is…

1a-03_s1_p2.text: The surgeon will perform the removal under anesthesia.

1a-03_s2_p0.text: -otomy is a cut; -ostomy is a new opening.

1a-03_s2_p1.text: A tracheotomy is an incision into the windpipe.

1a-03_s2_p2.text: A colostomy creates an opening from the colon.

1a-03_s4_p0.text: -scopy means looking inside with a camera.

1a-03_s4_p1.text: -gram is the image or recording you get.

1a-04_s3_p0.text: epi- means upon or on top of.

1a-04_s3_p2.text: A subcutaneous injection goes under the skin.

1a-04_s4_p2.text: An intravenous drip goes within a vein.

1a-04_q1.text: The sac AROUND the heart is the…

1a-04_q4.text: Decode: muscles BETWEEN the ribs are…

1a-05_s1_p2.text: Tachycardia is a rapid heart rate.

1a-05_s2_p1.text: Polyuria means passing a lot of urine.

1a-05_s2_p2.text: Polypharmacy is the use of many medicines.

1a-05_s3_p0.text: a-/an- means without or absence of.

1a-05_s3_p1.text: Apnea is the absence of breathing.

1a-05_s4_p2.text: An antipyretic brings down a fever.

1a-06_s1_p0.text: Cardiology is the study of the heart.

1a-06_s1_p1.text: Carditis is inflammation of the heart.

1a-06_s1_p2.text: Cardiomegaly is enlargement of the heart.

1a-06_s2_p0.text: vas/o and angi/o both mean vessel.

1a-06_s2_p2.text: An angiogram is an image of the vessels.

1a-06_s3_p0.text: tachy- + cardia = a fast heart rate.

1a-06_s3_p1.text: brady- + cardia = a slow heart rate.

1a-06_s3_p2.text: The prefix gives the rate, the root gives the organ.

1a-06_s4_p0.text: Cardiovascular means heart and vessels together.

1a-06_s4_p1.text: Myocardial means relating to the heart muscle.

1a-06_s4_p2.text: A myocardial infarction is a heart attack.

1a-06_q3.text: Build it: a SLOW heart rate is…

1a-07_s1_p0.text: pneum/o and pulmon/o both mean lung.

1a-07_s1_p1.text: Pneumonia is an infection of the lung.

1a-07_s1_p2.text: Pulmonary means relating to the lungs.

1a-07_s2_p1.text: A thoracotomy is an incision into the chest.

1a-07_s2_p2.text: Thoracic surgery operates on the chest.

1a-07_s3_p1.text: Dyspnea is difficult or laboured breathing.

1a-07_s3_p2.text: Dyspnea is the medical word for shortness of breath.

1a-07_s4_p1.text: Bronchitis is inflammation of the airways.

1a-07_s4_p2.text: A bronchoscopy looks inside the airways.

1a-08_s1_p1.text: Gastritis is inflammation of the stomach.

1a-08_s1_p2.text: A gastroscopy examines the stomach lining.

1a-08_s2_p1.text: Gastroenteritis is inflammation of the stomach and intestine.

1a-08_s2_p2.text: It often causes vomiting and diarrhea.

1a-08_s3_p1.text: Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver.

1a-08_s4_p1.text: A gastroenterologist treats stomach and intestine problems.

1a-08_s4_p2.text: Read it in pieces: gastr/o + enter/o + -logy.

1a-09_s1_p2.text: Neuropathy is disease of the nerves.

1a-09_s2_p2.text: You already saw it in 'myocardial' — heart muscle.

1a-09_s3_p0.text: oste/o means bone; arthr/o means joint.

1a-09_s3_p1.text: Arthritis is inflammation of a joint.

1a-09_s3_p2.text: Osteoporosis is an abnormal thinning of bone.

1a-09_s4_p0.text: An arthroscopy looks inside a joint with a camera.

1a-09_s4_p1.text: Osteoarthritis is wear of bone and joint.

1a-09_s4_p2.text: Read suffix first: -scopy = look, then arthr/o = joint.

1a-10_s1_p2.text: The posterior chest is the back of the chest.

1a-10_s2_p2.text: The heart is superior to the stomach.

1a-10_s3_p0.text: medial = toward the middle; lateral = toward the side.

1a-10_s3_p1.text: proximal = nearer the body; distal = farther from it.

1a-10_s3_p2.text: The fingers are distal to the wrist.

1a-10_s4_p1.text: Pericarditis is inflammation of the sac around the heart.

1a-10_q1.text: The wrist is ______ to the elbow.
